summary: Jeff Atwood shares his enthusiasm for Just Cause 2, an open-world sandbox game that he found unexpectedly addictive despite having limited gaming time while running Stack Overflow. He contrasts it favorably with Grand Theft Auto, which felt too much like work, praising Just Cause 2's pure chaos-driven gameplay where you literally advance the plot by blowing things up. The post also touches on the strong correlation between programmers and gamers, noting that gaming.stackexchange.com exceeded his expectations. Atwood recommends the game as an affordable holiday entertainment option.
key_points: There is a high correlation between programmers and gamers, evidenced by the success of gaming.stackexchange.com
Running your own business is 'crazy stupid' amounts of work, leaving little time for gaming or blogging
Just Cause 2 succeeds where GTA failed by being pure fun rather than feeling like work
The game's design philosophy of advancing plot through destruction creates uniquely entertaining emergent gameplay
The grappling hook and parachute mechanics transform simple traversal into creative experimentation
money_quotes: There is a high correlation between "programmer" and "gamer."
Running your own business is crazy stupid amounts of work when you turn it up to eleven.
Where GTA and its ilk often felt a tad too much like work for my tastes, Just Cause 2 is almost the opposite – it is non-stop, full blown open world pandemonium from start to finish.
One of the game's explicit goals is that you advance the plot by blowing stuff up.
Have you ever wondered what happens when you zipline a moving vehicle to a tree? Or a pedestrian? Or another vehicle?
Just Cause 2 is deliriously, stupidly, absurdly entertaining.
I spent my holidays on the beautiful island of Panau, and I don't regret a second of it.
key_insight: The best games, like the best software, respect the user's time by making the core experience inherently fun rather than padded with obligations that feel like work.
